note: EXAMPLE items demonstrating the dilemmas_v3 schema — schema/test fixture only, NOT the calibrated instrument. 2 items per cell (1 accepting / 1 active). Real candidates live in data/generated/dilemmas_v3_candidates.json.

dilemmas.situation: A close friend has died after a long illness. The funeral is over and everyday life is resuming.
dilemmas.stoic: Accept that the loss is permanent, and return attention to the responsibilities and people still present.
dilemmas.nonstoic: Keep the loss at the center of daily life, revisiting what could have been done differently.

dilemmas.situation: A manager takes credit for work a colleague quietly did. Speaking up would be uncomfortable and might not change anything.
dilemmas.stoic: State plainly and calmly who did the work, because saying what is true matters more than the discomfort.
dilemmas.nonstoic: Stay silent to protect the relationship with the manager and wait for a safer moment that may never come.

dilemmas.situation: An investment that took years of saving has lost most of its value in a market crash.
dilemmas.stoic: Treat the money as something held on loan from fortune, returned sooner than expected, and carry on with what remains.
dilemmas.nonstoic: Treat the loss as a wrong to be undone, and pour energy into recovering exactly what was lost.

dilemmas.situation: A neighborhood association needs a treasurer nobody wants to be; the role is thankless and the books are a mess.
dilemmas.stoic: Play your part: take the role fate has assigned and do it well, whether or not anyone applauds.
dilemmas.nonstoic: Decline, since the role brings work and blame without recognition, and let someone else absorb it.

dilemmas.situation: A release deadline was missed because a vendor shipped a broken dependency at the last minute.
dilemmas.stoic: Acknowledge the slip was outside the team's control, ship when the fix lands, and adjust the process going forward.
dilemmas.nonstoic: Keep relitigating whose fault the slip was and demand the schedule be treated as if the failure had not happened.

dilemmas.situation: A teammate is being unfairly benched by a coach who has clearly stopped watching their play.
dilemmas.stoic: Raise it directly with the coach, because pointing out the unfairness is the right thing to do even if it costs standing.
dilemmas.nonstoic: Say nothing and quietly benefit from the extra playing time the benching creates.

dilemmas.situation: An account that took years to build is suspended by a platform policy change that will not be reversed.
dilemmas.stoic: Regard the audience as having been on loan from the platform all along, now reclaimed, and build elsewhere without bitterness.
dilemmas.nonstoic: Fight the suspension indefinitely, since the audience was earned and is owed back.

dilemmas.situation: A lab's safety officer role is vacant and an experiment is scheduled; taking the role means paperwork and friction with colleagues.
dilemmas.stoic: Play your part and take the role the moment assigns, enforcing the rules evenly even when colleagues grumble.
dilemmas.nonstoic: Leave the role vacant, since the friction would land on whoever volunteers, and hope nothing goes wrong.
